ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic 'iced tea' class (FOODON_03301103); no specific entry for ice lemon tea or Singaporean-style lemon tea exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for ice lemon tea. AN000AJ (Tea, cold/iced) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept exists for ice lemon tea or Singaporean-style lemon tea beverage.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 300 ml (300 g), equivalent to a typical takeaway cup of ice lemon tea served at a Singapore kopitiam or casual dining restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Lemon tea, ready-to-drink / Iced tea with lemon — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy and HPB Singapore Foodo reference values for sweetened lemon tea
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service (FoodData Central) and Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Homemade ice lemon tea is not listed as a discrete entry in HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central entry for 'Tea, ready-to-drink, lemon flavour, sweetened' (FDC ID 171917) and cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo generic sweetened tea values. A typical homemade Singaporean ice lemon tea contains brewed black tea, fresh lemon juice, and added sugar (~6–8 g sugar per 100 ml). Per-100 g values reflect a lightly sweetened preparation consistent with kopitiam-style service.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of sweetened beverages and lemon tea analogues — University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS et al. (2021) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values 2021, Diabetes Care
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for homemade Singaporean ice lemon tea. GI estimated at 56 (medium) based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for sweetened iced tea / lemon-flavoured tea beverages with comparable sugar content (~6–8 g per 100 ml). Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (56 × 7.0 × 3) / 100 = 11.76, rounded to 11.8. Reference: Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021: a systematic review. Am J Clin Nutr. 2021;114(5):1625-1632.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (~4 mg per 100 g; ~12 mg per 300 ml serving)
hypertension.guidance: Homemade ice lemon tea is very low in sodium and poses minimal direct risk for hypertension; however, individuals should monitor total added sugar intake as high-sugar diets can indirectly contribute to elevated blood pressure over time.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Added sugar content (~6.8 g per 100 g; ~20 g per 300 ml serving) and medium glycemic index (~56)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit or avoid sweetened ice lemon tea due to its added sugar content and medium GI; requesting a reduced-sugar or sugar-free version and limiting portion size to 150–200 ml is advisable to manage postprandial blood glucose.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible fat and saturated fat; low sodium; moderate sugar
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Homemade ice lemon tea contains no fat or saturated fat and is very low in sodium, making it a low cardiovascular risk beverage in moderate quantities; however, regular consumption of the sweetened version should be moderated to avoid excess sugar intake contributing to dyslipidaemia and weight gain.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Homemade Ice Lemon Tea is a popular cold beverage widely consumed in Singapore, commonly served at kopitiams (traditional coffee shops), hawker centres, and casual dining establishments. It is prepared by brewing black tea (typically Ceylon or Lipton-style tea bags), allowing it to cool, then combining it with freshly squeezed or bottled lemon juice and sugar or simple syrup, and serving over crushed ice or ice cubes. The result is a refreshing, lightly tart, and moderately sweet drink with a pale amber colour and a clean citrus-tea aroma. The texture is thin and watery, characteristic of a cold tea beverage. In Singapore, ice lemon tea is a staple accompaniment to meals and is available at virtually every food court and hawker stall. The 'homemade' designation distinguishes it from commercially bottled lemon tea products (such as Pokka or Lipton RTD), indicating that the beverage is freshly prepared on-site. Nutritionally, a 300 ml serving provides approximately 84 kcal, primarily from added sugar (~20 g), with negligible protein and fat. The beverage is very low in sodium (~12 mg per serving) and contains a small amount of vitamin C from lemon juice (~7.5 mg per serving). Its medium glycemic index (~56) and moderate glycemic load (~11.8 per serving) make it a beverage that individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume in moderation or request in a reduced-sugar version. It is suitable for vegetarian, vegan, halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free diets.
common_user_queries: How many calories in ice lemon tea Singapore?
Is ice lemon tea good for diabetics?
What is the sugar content of ice lemon tea?
Is homemade ice lemon tea different from bottled lemon tea?
What is the GI of ice lemon tea?
Can I drink ice lemon tea if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for homemade Singaporean ice lemon tea exists in HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central sweetened ready-to-drink lemon tea (FDC ID 171917) and cross-referenced with HPB Singapore generic sweetened tea values. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for comparable sweetened tea beverages. Confidence is medium due to the adapted rather than exact source match.
notes: Fat_g is naturally zero as homemade ice lemon tea contains no fat-bearing ingredients (black tea, lemon juice, sugar, water, ice). Saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, and trans_fat_g are confirmed zero by formulation. Cholesterol_mg is confirmed zero by formulation. Dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as the beverage is a liquid with no fibre-bearing solids retained. Protein_g is 0.1 g per 100 g (trace from tea solids); this is not zero. Vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source (USDA FDC ID 171917) for this beverage category and is not measurable from the known ingredients at this dilution. Vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source (USDA FDC ID 171917) for this beverage category; black tea and lemon juice contribute negligible thiamine at typical serving concentrations. Vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source (USDA FDC ID 171917) for this beverage category; riboflavin content of brewed tea at this dilution is below reliable detection. Vitamin_b3_niacin_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source (USDA FDC ID 171917) for this beverage category; niacin from black tea at typical brew strength is not reliably quantified in the reference. Vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source (USDA FDC ID 171917) for this beverage category. Vitamin_b12_mcg is -1 as it is naturally zero in plant-based beverages and not reported in the adapted source; plant-derived beverages contain no B12. Folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source (USDA FDC ID 171917) for this beverage category; folate from lemon juice at typical dilution is not reliably quantified. Vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is naturally zero in this beverage and not reported in the adapted source; no vitamin D-bearing ingredients are present. Vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source (USDA FDC ID 171917) for this beverage category. Phosphorus_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source (USDA FDC ID 171917) for this beverage category. Magnesium_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source (USDA FDC ID 171917) for this beverage category. Zinc_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source (USDA FDC ID 171917) for this beverage category. Selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source (USDA FDC ID 171917) for this beverage category. GI set to 56 (medium) as an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for sweetened iced tea with comparable sugar content; no direct GI measurement for homemade Singaporean ice lemon tea was found. Glycemic load per 300 g serving calculated as (56 × 7.0 × 3) / 100 = 11.76, rounded to 11.8. Price data from field survey data (SGD 3.50) preserved from input record for reference only and not used as a nutritional source. FoodOn URI FOODON_03301103 and FoodEx2 code AN000AJ are used as closest parent matches; no exact ontology code exists for this specific beverage.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Built complete record from scratch. Added all mandatory Singaporean multilingual names (Chinese simplified/traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi). Adapted nutrient values from USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 171917 and HPB Singapore Foodo cross-reference. Set GI to 56 (medium) as analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database. Documented all zero-macro justifications in notes. Set fat_g zero justification as 'naturally zero'. Set dietary_fibre_g zero justification as 'naturally zero'. Documented all -1 sentinel values. Used closest_parent match_type for FoodOn and FoodEx2. Verified aliases_by_language consistency with flat aliases list.

Iteration 2: Addressed g3_numeric_hygiene hard gate failures by explicitly documenting the reason for each individual -1 sentinel value in the notes field. Previously a single sentence covered all -1 values; expanded to individual justifications per micronutrient.

Iteration 3: Resolved remaining g3_numeric_hygiene REJECT by rewriting each -1 sentinel value justification in the notes field to include explicit source attribution (USDA FDC ID 171917) and a specific reason why each micronutrient is not reported or not measurable for this beverage. Each of the 13 -1 fields (vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg) now has an individual, source-attributed justification sentence in the notes field.
